summary: Memorial Day in 1921 was the day everything changed for me. It was May 30th, early summer and getting warm, and I was only 18 years old. That morning seemed normal, but by afternoon, the Tulsa race massacre had erupted, forcing many of us to flee for our lives. I remember running off with many colored folks as the white mobs took over our part of the city.

Dick Rowland was a young man, about 19 years old, who worked shining shoes on the ground floor of the Drexel Building in Tulsa. On Memorial Day 1921, he took the elevator operated by Sarah Page, and an incident occurred that sparked the Tulsa race massacre. I didn't know him personally as I was younger and in grade school, but his arrest after the elevator incident led to the violent events that followed.

The Drexel Building at 319 S. Main in Tulsa was where Dick Rowland worked shining shoes. It was there on Memorial Day 1921 that the elevator incident with Sarah Page happened, which was the catalyst for the Tulsa race massacre. That building became part of the tragic history that day.

Sarah Page was the white elevator operator at the Drexel Building in Tulsa on Memorial Day 1921. She was 17 years old and the woman involved in the incident with Dick Rowland that led to his arrest. Her scream after he supposedly touched her arm was what ignited the tensions that exploded into the Tulsa race massacre.

Chicago was a city my father moved toward during the chaos of the Tulsa race massacre in 1921. He had left Tulsa and gone to the other side of Chicago, working on the Frisco Train. I tried to catch up with him during that time, but he had moved on from Chicago to Milwaukee, Wisconsin.

Milwaukee, Wisconsin was where my father worked for some leading white people on the Frisco Train after leaving Tulsa. I went there with my grandmother to try to find him during the Tulsa race massacre in 1921, but he had already left Milwaukee and moved on to California. It was a difficult journey for us, riding freight trains with no money.

Wisconsin was the state where Milwaukee is located, and where my father was working during the Tulsa race massacre in 1921. My grandmother and I traveled there to find him after fleeing Tulsa, but he had already left Wisconsin by the time we arrived. This was part of our attempt to escape the violence and chaos back home.

California was where my father eventually moved after leaving Milwaukee during the Tulsa race massacre in 1921. After learning he was there, my grandmother and I decided to try to go to California as well to find him and escape the violence in Tulsa. It was part of our effort to reunite and find safety.

The Tulsa Tribune was the afternoon newspaper on Memorial Day 1921 that published a headline accusing Dick Rowland of attacking a white girl in an elevator. It sensationalized the story, claiming he tore her clothes and scratched her face, and hinted at attempted rape. This inflammatory coverage helped fuel the racial violence that erupted that day.

Archer Street in Tulsa was where my father had built a home for my grandmother, not far from the Greenwood District. We lived there before the Tulsa race massacre, and I remember my grandmother had a garden there. It was close to the Frisco train tracks and near the area where the violence broke out.

The Greenwood District was our vibrant colored neighborhood in Tulsa before the massacre in 1921. We lived near there on Archer Street, and my cousin had a café way up on Greenwood. It was a prosperous area that the white mobs targeted out of jealousy and hatred during the Tulsa race massacre.

1st Street in Tulsa was where my mother worked downtown and where some of the sportin' women lived, just across from the Frisco train tracks. I used to carry groceries from my grandmother's garden over to them. It was a part of town that was close to where the racial violence erupted in 1921.

The Frisco Train was where my father worked as a Pullman car attendant, traveling through cities like Chicago, Milwaukee, and eventually California. It was a source of income and mobility for him during the turbulent times of the Tulsa race massacre in 1921. The train tracks also physically separated parts of Tulsa, like 1st Street from Archer Street.

The Jackson Funeral Home in Tulsa was where I was when the Tulsa race massacre broke out. A friend of mine, who was like a father to my sisters' children, was opening the garage there when he got shot in the hand. The funeral home was close to the front lines of the violence as the white mobs took over the colored parts of town.

The Verdigris River was a landmark we crossed on our way to Claremore during the Tulsa race massacre in 1921. After crossing it, white men stopped our car and searched us, but they let us go when they found we had no weapons. It was part of our escape route from the violence in Tulsa.

Claremore, Oklahoma was where my cousin and I went to escape the Tulsa race massacre in 1921. After fleeing Tulsa and being stopped near the Verdigris River, we made it to Claremore and stayed at a small hotel. Soldiers had come in to stop some of the violence, which allowed us to return to Tulsa the next morning, though everything had been burned down.

After the Tulsa race massacre destroyed much of the colored district, the Salvation Army came and built small buildings for the displaced colored folks to live in. My mother and others stayed in one of those little buildings because all the big homes and businesses had been burned down. It was a small comfort in the aftermath of the destruction.

The Tulsa race massacre on Memorial Day 1921 was a horrific event that changed my life forever. It started after the incident between Dick Rowland and Sarah Page at the Drexel Building, and the Tulsa Tribune's inflammatory headlines fueled the violence. White mobs attacked the prosperous colored district, forcing us to flee, burning homes including mine, and scattering families. I remember running from bullets, losing everything, and eventually seeking refuge in places like Claremore and the Salvation Army's temporary housing.
